quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Collision theory"?

options.text: Calculated the same way as K but with current concentrations; Q  K: reverse favored; Q = K: at equilibrium
π = iMRT; the pressure needed to prevent osmosis across a semipermeable membrane; used to determine molar mass of large molecules
Two or more reactants combine to form one product: A + B → AB (e.g., 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl)
A model stating reactions occur when molecules collide with sufficient energy (≥ Ea) and proper orientation

quiz_questions.explanation: "Collision theory" is defined as: A model stating reactions occur when molecules collide with sufficient energy (≥ Ea) and proper orientation. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Effective collision"?

options.text: A collision with enough energy (≥ Ea) and correct molecular orientation to break bonds and form products
Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by electron transfer; typically between metals and nonmetals (ΔEN > 1.7)
Separation based on density differences using rapid spinning; denser particles settle to the bottom (pellet), lighter components remain in solution (supernatant)
A strong dipole-dipole interaction between H bonded to N, O, or F and a lone pair on another N, O, or F atom

quiz_questions.explanation: "Effective collision" is defined as: A collision with enough energy (≥ Ea) and correct molecular orientation to break bonds and form products. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution"?

options.text: Properties that depend on the number of dissolved particles, not their chemical identity: ΔTb, ΔTf, π, ΔP
The distribution of molecular kinetic energies at a given temperature; a higher temperature shifts the peak and increases the fraction of molecules above Ea
ΔH ≈ Σ(bonds broken) - Σ(bonds formed); positive ΔH if more energy is needed to break bonds than is released forming new ones
Increasing pressure (decreasing volume) shifts equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas; inert gas at constant V has no effect

quiz_questions.explanation: "Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution" is defined as: The distribution of molecular kinetic energies at a given temperature; a higher temperature shifts the peak and increases the fraction of molecules above Ea. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Steric factor"?

options.text: ΔTf = iKfm; solute particles lower the freezing point by disrupting crystal formation
A proton (H⁺) acceptor
The fraction of collisions with the correct orientation for reaction; determines the pre-exponential factor A in the Arrhenius equation
Strong acids dissociate 100% (Ka very large); weak acids partially dissociate (Ka < 1, typically 10⁻² to 10⁻¹⁰)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Steric factor" is defined as: The fraction of collisions with the correct orientation for reaction; determines the pre-exponential factor A in the Arrhenius equation. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Transition state (activated complex)"?

options.text: Acid: produces H⁺(aq); base: produces OH⁻(aq); limited to aqueous solutions
The high-energy, unstable intermediate configuration at the top of the energy barrier between reactants and products
Acid: electron pair acceptor; base: electron pair donor; the broadest definition, encompassing reactions without proton transfer
The ONLY disturbance that changes K; increasing T favors the endothermic direction; decreasing T favors the exothermic direction

quiz_questions.explanation: "Transition state (activated complex)" is defined as: The high-energy, unstable intermediate configuration at the top of the energy barrier between reactants and products. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A model stating reactions occur when molecules collide with sufficient energy (≥ Ea) and proper orientation"?

options.text: Collision theory
Viscosity
Hess's Law
Integrated rate law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Collision theory".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A collision with enough energy (≥ Ea) and correct molecular orientation to break bonds and form products"?

options.text: Calorimetry
Equilibrium vs. completion
Effective collision
Diprotic acid

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Effective collision".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The distribution of molecular kinetic energies at a given temperature; a higher temperature shifts the peak and increases the fraction of molecules above Ea"?

options.text: Side reaction
pKa
Factors affecting rate
Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The fraction of collisions with the correct orientation for reaction; determines the pre-exponential factor A in the Arrhenius equation"?

options.text: Conjugate acid-base pair
Selective precipitation
Kw (autoionization constant)
Steric factor

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Steric factor".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The high-energy, unstable intermediate configuration at the top of the energy barrier between reactants and products"?

options.text: Indicator
Standard reduction potential (E°)
Transition state (activated complex)
Secondary battery

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Transition state (activated complex)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Collision Theory is accurate?

options.text: Kc uses molar concentrations, Kp uses partial pressures for gases (Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn).
Collision Theory explains reaction rates at the molecular level: for a reaction to occur, molecules must (1) collide with sufficient energy (≥ activation energy, Ea) and (2) have proper orientation.
Solids and Liquids exhibit distinct properties based on their intermolecular forces and particle arrangements.
Entropy increases when: solids melt or liquids vaporize, gases expand, temperature increases, or the number of particles increases.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Collision Theory as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Collision Theory?

options.text: Balanced equations provide mole ratios for calculating how much product forms from given reactants, or how much reactant is needed.
The 5% approximation simplifies calculations when [HA]₀/Ka > 400.
Intermolecular Forces (IMFs) are attractions between molecules that determine physical properties like boiling point, viscosity, and surface tension.
Only a small fraction of collisions are effective.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Collision Theory as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Collision Theory?

options.text: Equilibrium Constants quantify the position of equilibrium.
The Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution shows the range of molecular kinetic energies at a given temperature; increasing temperature shifts the distribution so more molecules exceed Ea.

Solutions and Concentration describes how solutes dissolve in solvents through solvation. 'Like dissolves like': polar solvents dissolve polar/ionic solutes, nonpolar solvents dissolve nonpolar solutes.
Increasing pressure shifts toward the side with fewer moles of gas.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Collision Theory as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Collision Theory?

options.text: Ion-dipole forces are the strongest IMF and govern dissolution of ionic compounds in polar solvents.

Acid-Base Definitions have evolved from Arrhenius (acids produce H⁺, bases produce OH⁻ in water) to Brønsted-Lowry (acids donate H⁺, bases accept H⁺) to Lewis (acids accept electron pairs, bases donate electron pairs).
Balanced equations provide mole ratios for calculating how much product forms from given reactants, or how much reactant is needed.
Collision Theory explains reaction rates at the molecular level: for a reaction to occur, molecules must (1) collide with sufficient energy (≥ activation energy, Ea) and (2) have proper orientation.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Collision Theory as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Collision Theory?

options.text: Acid-Base Definitions have evolved from Arrhenius (acids produce H⁺, bases produce OH⁻ in water) to Brønsted-Lowry (acids donate H⁺, bases accept H⁺) to Lewis (acids accept electron pairs, bases donate electron pairs).
Only a small fraction of collisions are effective.
Zero-order: rate is independent of concentration; first-order: rate doubles when concentration doubles; second-order: rate quadruples when concentration doubles.
Activation Energy and Catalysts are central to understanding and controlling reaction rates.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Collision Theory as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Collision Theory?

options.text: Kidney stone formation: when [Ca²⁺][C₂O₄²⁻] exceeds Ksp of calcium oxalate (2.3 × 10⁻⁹), crystals precipitate in the kidney
Hemoglobin-oxygen binding equilibrium shifting in lungs (high O₂, favoring HbO₂) vs. tissues (low O₂, favoring O₂ release)
Biological research: phosphate buffer (H₂PO₄⁻/HPO₄²⁻, pKa = 7.20) commonly used in cell culture media to maintain physiological pH
Match striking requires friction to provide activation energy, initiating the exothermic combustion reaction of KClO₃ and sulfur in the match head

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Collision Theory.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Collision Theory?

options.text: Ammonia's pyramidal geometry (sp³, one lone pair) making it a strong Lewis base and critical industrial chemical in fertilizer production (Haber process)
Temperature's exponential effect on reaction rates: a 10°C increase approximately doubles the rate because of the exponential increase in molecules above Ea
Forensic chemistry using stoichiometric analysis to determine the composition of unknown substances from mass spectral data
Vinegar concentration determination: titrating with standardized NaOH; the acetic acid content must be 4-8% by mass for commercial vinegar

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Collision Theory.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Collision Theory applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Enzyme catalysis lowering Ea for biochemical reactions, allowing them to proceed rapidly at body temperature (37°C) rather than requiring extreme heating
Stoichiometric calculations in pharmaceutical manufacturing ensuring precise drug composition, maintaining consistent dosage and efficacy
Industrial removal of products: in esterification (alcohol + acid ⇌ ester + water), removing water with molecular sieves shifts equilibrium toward ester, increasing yield
Forensic analysis using gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) to identify and quantify drugs, poisons, and accelerants in criminal investigations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Collision Theory.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Collision Theory?

options.text: Industrial process design using Hess's Law to predict energy requirements for multi-step chemical syntheses without performing every reaction experimentally
Solar water heating systems using water's high specific heat capacity to absorb and store thermal energy efficiently
Carbonated beverages: dissolved CO₂ forms carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), a diprotic acid giving soft drinks their tangy taste (pH ~3-4)
Match striking requires friction to provide activation energy, initiating the exothermic combustion reaction of KClO₃ and sulfur in the match head

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Collision Theory.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Collision Theory in practice?

options.text: The spontaneous rusting of iron: despite being exothermic (ΔH < 0), rust formation is also driven by the entropy increase from solid Fe converting to flaky Fe₂O₃
Catalytic converters using Pt, Pd, and Rh heterogeneous catalysts to lower Ea for converting exhaust pollutants (CO, NOₓ) to harmless products
Temperature's exponential effect on reaction rates: a 10°C increase approximately doubles the rate because of the exponential increase in molecules above Ea
Quality control in fuel production: measuring heat of combustion per gram to verify fuel grade and energy content

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Collision Theory.
